stat_justification.stf: Elite curveball — 'big sweeping curveball that would freeze batters.' 'Great speed, sharp curves.' Held NL hitters to .210 BA against (1908). 915 career K. Sent to minors specifically to learn to disguise his curve — and it worked. Rating of 3.
stat_justification.ctl: 1.56 ERA (1908), 1.62 ERA (1909) — elite results. But walked his way through the 5-inning no-hitter. 'Control proved to be Camnitz's biggest challenge.' Good overall pitching intelligence — studied box scores to learn hitters' streaks — but the control wavered. Rating of 2.
stat_justification.sta: 283 IP (1909). 240+ IP seven consecutive years. 137 career CG. Solid durability during peak. But 5'9" 165 lbs — slight frame that wore down. Sharp decline after 1912. Conditioning issues throughout. Rating of 2.
stat_justification.clu: BOMBED in the 1909 World Series. Lost Game 2 to Detroit. 'Did not perform well, speculation he was either ill or drinking.' Manager Fred Clarke bypassed his 25-game-winning ace and started rookie Babe Adams in Games 1, 5, and 7. Adams won all three. Camnitz — the ace — was not trusted when it mattered. 1× WS champion but as a spectator. Rating of 0.

personality.leadership_style: THE ANALYST. 'I always inspect very closely the box score of the club we are about to meet next. My object is to ascertain what players are doing the hitting.' Camnitz studied the game analytically — tracking hitters' hot and cold streaks, deciding when to challenge and when to pitch around. An intelligent pitcher on the mound. Less intelligent off it.
personality.temperament: CHARMING BUT UNRELIABLE. 'Rosebud' — the nickname fit the personality. Bright, colorful, sociable. 'A penchant for socializing, never in the best of shape.' The red hair and the charm masked a man who couldn't resist the social scene. Good company, questionable commitment. Honus Wagner called him 'a troublemaker' during the Federal League recruitment scandal.
personality.work_ethic: CONTRADICTORY. On the mound: 283 IP, 20 CG, 25 wins. The work was there. Off the mound: conditioning issues, drinking speculation, socializing, poor shape. Released in 1915 for 'violating team rules.' The arm was willing. The body was not always prepared.
personality.dark_side: The 1909 World Series. Camnitz won 25 games. He was the ace. And Clarke didn't trust him for the clinching games. 'Ill or drinking' — the speculation has never been confirmed, but the result is clear: rookie Babe Adams started Games 1, 5, and 7 and won all three. The 25-game winner watched. The championship was won by someone else. The Rosebud wilted in October. The same charm that made him 'Rosebud' may have been the drinking that made him unreliable.

chemistry_traits.desc: Bright red hair. Covington, KY. Centre College. Charming, sociable, colorful. +2 personality. The nickname that captured both the beauty and the fragility.
'Big sweeping curveball that would freeze batters in their tracks.' Sent to minors to learn to disguise it. When it worked: 1.56 ERA, .210 BAA. +3 STF.
'I always inspect the box score of the club we meet next.' Studied hitters' streaks. Knew when to challenge, when to pitch around. +2 intelligence.
25 wins, .806 W%. Clarke started rookie Babe Adams instead. Adams won 3 games. Camnitz lost Game 2 and was shelved. -3 CLU. The ace the manager didn't trust.
1909 WS: 'speculation he was either ill or drinking.' Never confirmed. Conditioning issues plagued career. -2 reliability. The speculation that shadows the Rosebud.
1914: jumped to Federal League. Tried recruiting Pirates players in hotel lobby. Wagner called him 'a troublemaker.' -1 loyalty.
After baseball: automobile salesman in Louisville for 40 years. The charming personality that served him on the mound served him in the showroom. +1 post-career.

hot_triggers: Regular season dominance — 25-6, .806 W%. When healthy and focused, Camnitz was elite.
The curveball working — when the big sweeping curve was biting, batters froze.
Studying opponents — his box score analysis gave him an edge in preparation.
Early season form — Camnitz opened 1909 with a shutout on Opening Day.
cold_triggers: October pressure — bombed in 1909 WS, bypassed by manager for clinching games.
Conditioning lapses — 'never in the best of shape.' The socializing caught up.
Post-peak decline — sharp dropoff after 1912. Released for violating team rules in 1915.
Trust erosion — once Clarke bypassed him, the confidence never fully returned.
momentum.pressure_response: COLLAPSED. The 1909 World Series defines Camnitz's pressure response: the 25-game winner who lost Game 2 and was shelved for the clinching games. 'Ill or drinking' — whatever the cause, Clarke didn't trust his ace when it mattered most. Rookie Babe Adams won 3 games instead. In ILB: Camnitz is a regular-season ace with a postseason collapse trait. The Rosebud wilts in October.

action_card_seeds.text: Your pitcher finishes 1909 with a 25-6 record and a 1.62 ERA. He ties Christy Mathewson for the NL lead in winning percentage at .806. He throws 283 innings, 20 complete games, 6 shutouts. He shuts out the Reds on Opening Day. He is the ace of the pennant-winning Pirates. +3 dominance. +2 wins. The regular season belongs to the Rosebud.

action_card_seeds.title: The October Collapse
action_card_seeds.text: World Series Game 2. Your ace takes the mound. Detroit hits him. He loses. There is speculation he is 'ill or drinking.' Your manager shelves him. For the clinching games, your manager starts a 12-3 rookie named Babe Adams instead. Adams wins Games 1, 5, and 7. Your 25-game winner watches. The Pirates are champions. Your ace is not the reason. -3 CLU. -2 trust. The Rosebud wilts.
action_card_seeds.origin: 1909 WS: Camnitz lost G2, bypassed for Adams in clinching games.

action_card_seeds.title: The Curveball That Froze Them
action_card_seeds.text: Your pitcher's curveball arrives. The batter doesn't move. The umpire raises his fist. Frozen. 'Big sweeping curveball that would freeze batters in their tracks.' In 1908, NL hitters bat .210 against him. The ERA: 1.56. +3 STF. The pitch they sent him to the minors to disguise — and it became the pitch that made him.
action_card_seeds.origin: Camnitz was sent to minors to disguise his curve. Mastered it.

action_card_seeds.title: The Troublemaker at the Hotel
action_card_seeds.text: 1914. Federal League. Your former pitcher shows up at the Pirates' hotel in Hot Springs during spring training. He's recruiting your players. Dreyfuss gets a court injunction. Honus Wagner tells him to stay away from the younger players. 'A troublemaker.' The Rosebud has thorns. -1 loyalty. -1 clubhouse.
action_card_seeds.origin: 1914: Camnitz tried to recruit Pirates players for Federal League.

art_direction.face: SLIGHT, RED-HAIRED, CHARMING, UNRELIABLE. 5'9" 165 lbs — small for a pitcher, smaller than most Muggers. The face should be BRIGHT and SOCIABLE — this is 'Rosebud,' a man of charm and color. Red hair visible under the cap. Pleasant features, quick smile — the face of a man who was good company and knew it. But the eyes should carry a hint of UNRELIABILITY — something slightly unfocused, slightly too relaxed. Not a drunkard's face — a SOCIALIZER's face. The man who might have been out too late the night before the World Series.
art_direction.mood: BRIGHT SURFACE, HOLLOW CENTER. The card should feel CHARMING but UNRELIABLE — the beauty of the Rosebud and the fragility. Not tragic like Alexander or haunted like Merkle — DISAPPOINTING. The card of a man who could have been great but wasn't quite.
art_direction.style: Sepia-toned with KENTUCKY BLUEGRASS GREEN and ROSEBUD PINK-RED — the green of the Bluegrass country he came from, the pinkish-red of his nickname and his hair. The BRIGHTEST pitcher palette — colorful, vivid, the opposite of Alexander's weighted gray. The color of promise.
